comment: Config task, no Lua touched — the map tooling was never exercised. Located the commit skill with find+grep over .claude/, which map/ does not index and should not.

The load-bearing question was a data-flow one the index cannot express: which paths mutate the notes/ccs arrays directly rather than through the verbs (answer: resolveCollisions and load dedup). map_query has no way to ask what writes a given @state; I found them by grepping `dirty = true` and `notes[...] = nil`. The .map @state lines with their trailing comments were the fastest way to read indexStale semantics, and map/specs/*.map gave me the spec case-lists in one shot.

Mostly went around the maps. The hunt was intra-file — trackerManager file-local closures (mmBatch, idxReconcile, chansInsert, makeEntry) — and .map indexes module surface, not locals, so grep_window was the only tool that could find callers; map_query earned its keep only twice, for mm:modify and mm:poolGuid. The real gap: nothing models lexical scope. The bug was `deferredSort` written at :1515 while its `local` at :608 lives in a do-block that closes near :1200, so the write silently bound a global and the block-local read never saw it. A map that recorded do-block extents — or merely flagged a name referenced outside its declaring block — would have named this in one query, instead of five REAPER instrumentation round-trips.

Never opened a map. Two reasons, one mine and one the tooling's. Mine: I knew the files, so I went straight to grep_window/multi_read for exact ranges -- and then guessed tm:channel() twice in a bridge chunk when map/trackerManager.map would have told me it is tm:channels() returning an iterator. The tooling's: the load-bearing question of this session was "who subscribes to mm's reload signal?" -- the emits side is indexed (--emits:), the subscribe side is not, and mm:subscribe(name, fn) is a runtime-receiver call so usedby drops it. Had to grep. An index of subscribe(signal) call sites, keyed by signal name, would make the emits edges bidirectional and answer "what breaks if I change this signal's fire conditions" in one query.

map_query(kind='usedby', module='eventMeta') silently returned eventMeta's OUTBOUND edges, not its callers -- the module filter scopes to the map file an entry lives in, so it and usedby are contradictory and the query answers the wrong question rather than erroring. Wasted a call and I nearly believed it. Either reject the combination or make module mean 'callers in this module'. Separately: I greped tests/specs for sweep|purge to find whether the stale-uuid sweep was covered, when kind='case' query='*metadata*' would likely have found 'loser metadata purged' in one shot -- the spec-map case index is there and I didn't reach for it, so that one's on me.

map_query(kind='usedby', query='cm:get') returned a flat 'no matches' — cm:get has ~100 call sites across 20 prod files. Same runtime-receiver gap as last session's module-filter bug, but the failure mode is worse here: for a shared service, 'who calls this' is THE query before a refactor, and a silent confident 'no callers' is the one wrong answer that could get someone hurt. Consider erroring, or degrading to a textual scan, rather than returning empty. Credit where due: reading map/configManager.map directly earned its keep — the `@use call util.assign @ mergedTable:377` edge told me in one line that deleting mergedTable orphans the file's only util.assign use, which I'd otherwise have grepped for.

Docs-archive task: moved 3 design docs and repointed 31 inbound `see design/x.md` pointers. Deliberately null, not 3 — the map tooling was never the right instrument here and shouldn't be scored for it. Worth noting as a gap all the same: .map indexes symbols, not doc pointers, so 'what cites design/dirty-channels.md' is grep-only, and there's no way to ask 'which doc references now dangle'. My ad-hoc check (extract every design/*.md ref, test -f each) found 11 PRE-EXISTING broken links from earlier archive moves that nobody caught. A link-integrity check over the docs graph would have caught those on the commit that broke them; if the map generator ever indexes `see docs/...`/`design/...` pointers, that check comes free.

map_query module dump gave the whole tm fn/api index in one call — ideal for locating the design doc's line refs (:2215, :2332, etc). grep_window found the 7 note-column insertion sites cleanly. No defect; usedby not needed this pass.

map_query fn/api dump kept me oriented across a wide tm change. Gap: no way to ask the map 'how many times per rebuild is this column sorted / which stages call sortByPPQL' — a call-count-in-pipeline view would have surfaced the 4x-redundant-sort finding without hand-tracing the pipeline order.

Intra-file dedup (drop one of four note-column sorts). Knew the file and the symbol, so grep_window over trackerManager.lua found the 4 sortByPPQL sites + pipeline order in one pass; Read confirmed rebuildFx sits immediately after computeFxWindows. Didn't reach for map_query — correct for single-file navigation, wouldn't have added anything. Reinforces the prior line's gap: the map can't answer 'how many times per rebuild is a column sorted / which stages call sortByPPQL', the exact fact this change turned on; I hand-traced the pipeline to prove only rebuildFx's sort was redundant.

Deep intra-file audit: 'which functions read note columns in ppqL order between two pipeline points, and are they host/window-gated'. grep_window over trackerManager.lua enumerated the readers (eachWindowNote/membersOf/channelStreams/forEachEvent/rebuildRegionPark) and Read classified each. Real gap the map can't close: this is a data-flow + pipeline-ordering question. usedby gives callers but not 'reads structure X in array order', and nothing expresses 'runs between stage A and stage B of rebuildPipeline'. I hand-traced pipeline order from the source. A 'reads-in-order' edge or a pipeline-sequence view would have turned a 6-read audit into one query.

live bridge-authoring task needed exact shapes not locations — generators.kinds defaults, addEvent per-evType accepted fields (cc/pa/at), markGroup rect — so went straight to source via grep_window. A map_query surfacing a module data-table of defaults (generators.kinds) would have saved 3-4 reads.
map_query resolved the voicing shape-peer cleanly in one call; rest of the session was new-file authoring (intervals.lua) plus a fixture edit, little to index against

map_query cleanly located the low-level verbs (addLowlevel/assignLowlevel/deleteLowlevel) and the intervals API in one call each; no gaps this session. Source-range reads still went through multiread/grep_window, as expected.
map_query rebuild on tm was the whole orientation pass - stage fns with ranges plus contracts/invariants in one call; niggle: annotation hits print file-only, no line to jump to

core task was finding every ds:get(key) read inside the pipeline stages — a text sweep grep_window handled well; map_query gave the stage fn ranges but cannot express dataStore-key reads as a queryable edge, so the actual survey was all grep

Meta-session: the map tooling WAS the task. Reading trackerManager.map directly cracked it — the `@use call cm.get` line showed chunk callers stored cm:get under the unresolved dep-name while patternEditor (construct) stored configManager.get, so a module's callers split across two keys and usedby never unified them. Second find: usedby was never a reverse index — it ran the SAME loop as uses, so usedby+module returned the module's OWN outbound edges (the back-to-front the feedback log flagged twice). Fixed both: generator now stores receivers source-faithfully (cm:get, colon kept, no cross-file resolution — keeps per-file regen pure), and the query resolves short names via the self= registry, accepts all four spellings, and usedby is a true reverse scan with module= naming the used target. map_query couldn't be exercised live (running server holds old code); verified new logic by importing the module against real maps.

Closed feedback line 15's own complaint: annotation hits (invariant/contract/shape/reaper) now emit file:line, so you can jump to them. As an instrument this session, grep over map_extract.py found the emit paths (fmt_ann / attach_annotations / shape block) faster than any map query could — the generator's own structure is not indexed by the maps it produces, which is correct and fine. Reading trackerManager.map directly confirmed the new `@ <line>` suffix and that header annotations (@deps) stay lineless. The line was already carried to pending_annotations and dropped only at attach; shape_annotations already kept a 4-tuple, so the fix was widening every annotation to match. No new gap surfaced.

map_query gave the rebuild* stage roster with line numbers in one call — clean entry point. The per-field producer/consumer split of the fx bag came from grep_window over fx.<field>, not the index; that's field-level dataflow, below map granularity, so expected rather than a gap.

map_query strong for production nav (annotations, api, mmBatch). Two edges: a |-alternation query on kind=api matched nothing (it's glob not regex, so I re-ran with * and filtered); and the test harness (harness.lua/fakeReaper.lua) isn't in the index, so every harness-surface question fell back to grep/read.
map/bridge.map located render/parseDirectives/DEFAULTS with line ranges in one read; grepped bridge.lua only to confirm the truncation call sites. Didn't need map_query — single-file change.
fn-range queries anchored every read precisely; usedby on a file-local fn (findNoteColumnForPitch) returns nothing, so local call sites still need a grep fallback
fn-range queries anchored every read; the audit question "who reads field X off column events" is inexpressible in the maps -- field-level reads need grep + hand-filtering frames

never opened map_query: the whole task was "who reads field X, and in which frame" -- maps index symbols, not fields, and never the provenance of a local. Cost a real bug: I classified setLength/rescaleLength as mm-frame readers because grep shows the line, not that their forEachEvent yields *column* events; only the suite caught it. An edge for "what does this iterator yield" / "which fns consume columns" would have paid for itself

needed 'what WRITES field ppqL' — grep on .ppqL gave 40 hits with reads/writes undifferentiated, and the one that mattered (the CC walk's foreign-MIDI stamp) was 40th-ish; a write-site filter would have ended the question in one call. Also: the two facts that settled it lived in prose (a harness comment, a spec header), which no map indexes — the spec maps carry intent/cases but not 'this fixture exists because X stamps Y'. Bypassed map_query entirely: the question was about frames and provenance, not location.

every decisive question this session was ordering/reachability across subscriber wiring -- does absorbReloadDirt fold before tm:rebuild's didReload widens it back, can a flush land between markSwingStale and the rebuild that consumes it. `uses` gives edges but not sequence, so all of it went to grep. also: design/audit/*.md described a tm that no longer exists and nothing flagged the drift

never opened map_query this session -- the decisive questions were all field-level dataflow, which the index doesn't carry. 'what reads endppqC' was the one that mattered (it falsified a claim I was about to write into a design doc: endppqC turned out to feed a producer via soundingCell and four tv edit verbs, not just paint). usedby resolves methods, not field reads, so that went to grep_window and would have gone there whatever the maps said. same for 'who calls intervals.close' -- one grep, one answer. maps are for locating code; this session was about who depends on a value.

kind='case' + query='*same*pitch*' across module='*_spec' surfaced all 15 candidate specs in one call — exactly the lookup I'd have spent five greps on. The gap was field-level reverse lookup: the load-bearing question was 'who consumes realiseParked's endppqC', and usedby resolves symbols, not the fields a function writes, so I grep_window'd for it and found the second consumer (a generator window) that the maps couldn't have told me about.

map_query kind='usedby' is call-site shaped, and this session's central question was field shaped. Migrating events off .token means finding every READ of evt.token -- tv's locs[e.token], clipboard/gm strip lists, tm's staging records -- not every call to mm:byToken. usedby drops field accesses, so I swept with grep_window over *.lua instead and it's what actually produced the blast radius. Same field-level gap I logged on 2026-07-16 from the other side (who consumes realiseParked's endppqC); twice in two sessions suggests it's structural, not bad luck. The one thing that did land cleanly: kind='shape' annotations carried the load-bearing fact (cc's [uuid] is optional, note's is not), though I reached it by grep rather than by asking for it.

A Lua-heavy session in which I never once reached for map_query or a .map -- grep_window + multi_read did all of it. Third session running the reason is the same: the load-bearing question was field-shaped, not call-site shaped. 'What breaks if evt.token stops meaning content position' resolves to reads of evt.token (tv's locs[e.token], the clipboard/gm strip lists, tm's staging records, pb.origTok) and to sites that CONJURE a token from bare fields (tm:974, tm:2126) -- neither is a call to mm:byToken, and usedby indexes calls. I missed tm:2126 on my own grep sweep and only found it by tracing a red spec, which is exactly the recall gap an index should close. A kind='reads' over field accesses, or 'usedby' widened to field-level, would have produced the blast radius in one call. Logged from the other side on 2026-07-16 (who consumes realiseParked's endppqC) and again in step 1; three times in three sessions is structural.

Second half of the uuid migration: a repo-wide rename pass, and the map tooling has no purchase on that shape at all -- never opened it. A rename operates on file-local identifiers and field names (byToken, origTok, evt.token, the local byUuid), and map_query indexes module-level symbols and call edges, so it can neither enumerate the sites nor tell me when I'd missed one. I went to grep and got burned: my survey pattern was lowercase 'token', byToken has a capital T, two sites hid, and I shipped a global-leak (loadIndex assigning a global byToken, the flush scan iterating it empty) that the 2040-test suite passed straight over. Only a hand-written verification grep caught it. The ask isn't a new query so much as a symbol table with file-local scope: 'every read/write of identifier X in file Y, case-exact, including field accesses' is the whole job of a rename, and nothing here answers it. Third consecutive session logging the same field-level recall gap.

spec maps earned their keep: @harness/@surface/@intent across three candidates let me pick tm_clear_same_key_spec as the shape to copy without opening any of them, and mm_collision_backstop_spec.map's intent block told me tm separates collisions before mm's backstop sees them -- which turned out to be the load-bearing fact of the session. The module map was absent for the actual question, which was frame-shaped: 'does this comparison read .ppq or .ppqL' is expression-level and nothing indexes it, so the whole investigation ran on grep_window. Also grepped for callers of forEachAttachedPA -- a file-local private helper, so usedby has no entry for it; a file-local call graph would have answered it in one call instead of three.

map_query with kind=case + module='*_spec' was the clean win: 'which specs exercise reswing' in one call, and it surfaced the two specs whose disagreement became the session's real finding. Everything else went around the maps. The costly gap was fixture shape, not structure: two specs declare the same swing curve differently -- vm_reswing_cc_spec's {factors={...}} vs vm_reswing_lane_stability_spec's bare list, which silently resolves to identity -- and I copied the broken one, then burned four probe rounds on a scenario that wasn't swinging at all. Spec maps index @harness/@intent/@case but not the fixture literals a spec builds, so nothing could surface that two specs disagree on the shape of the same config value; a @fixture kind over the seed/config tables would have. Separately, no index answers 'what else would still cover this if I deleted X', which is the question every deletion asks -- that one took disabling code and re-running, and probably always will.

Sequel to yesterday's last entry, which logged getting burned by the bare-list swing fixture -- the user came back and killed the spec it produced. The win was the module map read as prose: one grep of map/trackerManager.map for 'lane' surfaced two @contract lines ('pick a lane for an external (unstamped) note' @1797, 'consulted only for unstamped raw notes; stamped notes never reach it' @1770) and that pair IS the finding -- lane drift is unreachable for stamped notes, so the spec was dead whatever its fixture said. Never called map_query; grepping the .map directly was faster than composing a query for what amounted to 'show me every annotation mentioning lane'. One real defect: tm:802's @contract cites pickStampedLane, which does not exist in trackerManager.lua -- docs/trackerManager.md:245 and :330 repeat the name. The map faithfully reproduces a stale annotation, so a dangling function name now has three homes and reads as authoritative in all of them; I chased it for a call before concluding it was a ghost. The @fixture ask from yesterday's entry earned itself again: the malformed literal was in four spec files, not one, and nothing but a hand-rolled grep for atom='classic' lines lacking 'factors' could enumerate them -- indexing the literals a spec's seed/config tables build would have made that a query. Deletion coverage remains unanswerable by any index; I fixed the fixture in a scratch copy and re-ran to prove the spec was vacuous, same as last time.

Meta-session (built the field index, regex queries, harness maps), so the tooling was subject more than instrument. Two defects only verification queries caught: fakeReaper's whole surface was invisible — `function r.X(...)` inside new() is neither @api (owner ≠ return target) nor a dot-pass write — until declarations were made to count as field writes; and the self= registry silently last-wins on collisions (fakeReaper and support both return a local M) — ambiguous names now drop. One usage lesson: I burned a query asking kind='api' for bridge's render/parseDirectives, which are @fn — when unsure of kind, omit it. Empty results doubled as the session's best defect detector: 'writes ppqL module=midiManager' returned nothing, and the source confirmed the CC-walk stamp was refactored away — the index told the truth.

Never reached for the maps: the report I was working from carried exact file:line anchors, so grep_window and multi_read went straight there. Scoring null rather than a polite 3. Worth logging the gap the task did expose -- the load-bearing falsehood was in docs/trackerManager.md, a paragraph asserting that resizeNote 'shifts a PA in both frames, so raw and intent never drift apart' when the code had stopped doing that. Nothing indexes doc prose against the code it describes, so a doc that goes stale is invisible to every tool here; the .map is derived from source and agrees with it by construction, which means it can never catch the disagreement that matters. No idea what the query would look like, but the failure mode is real and I have now hit it twice.

Bypassed the maps again, and this time for a structural reason rather than convenience. The load-bearing question was 'who reads collisionIdx?' -- a file-local table read by three file-local functions. map_query's uses/usedby is a cross-module method graph resolved through the alias table, so a private table and its private readers simply have no representation in it; the only sound way to prove the cc half dead was to exhaust all 29 references with grep over the single file. That is not a defect in how the index answers, it is a gap in what it indexes: nothing here models intra-module data flow, so 'is this private field written but never read' -- the exact shape of dead-weight findings -- is unaskable. Scoring null rather than penalise the tooling for a question it never claimed to answer, but two nulls running is itself a signal about which questions this work actually generates.

Wiring up luacheck; never touched the maps, so null rather than a polite 3. Reason is structural and probably permanent for this task shape: a linter's output IS a coordinate list, so every target arrived pre-resolved as file:line and grep_window/multi_read went straight there -- there was no 'where does X live' question left for an index to answer. But the task exposed a lookup the maps should have owned and I did by hand. The whole pass hinged, repeatedly, on one judgement luacheck cannot make: is this unused argument a defect, or a parameter of a declared uniform signature? That is exactly an annotation question. generators.lua:6's --invariant declares 'a stage is fn(stream, host, params, ctx)', which is the entire reason 8 unused `host` args are correct code; viewContext.lua:49's --contract says outright that `chan` is 'kept for the call-site signature'. I found both by grepping source. Reading map/generators.map would have answered it in one shot, and I did not think to -- the linter had already told me the line number, so I never asked what the module claimed about itself. Lesson is mine, not the tool's. The tool-shaped version of the gap: nothing lets you ask 'which params are protocol' -- the invariant states the signature in prose, so matching it against an actual arg list is beyond what the index encodes, and a --signature: kind that named protocol params would have made a 35-warning judgement call a query.

Shadowing pass over luacheck's 35 warnings. Null again, second entry running -- but this time it is half my fault and half a real gap, and the two are worth separating. My miss: the pass turned on 'is renderBody(ctx, w, h, dispatch) a uniform page interface, or is editorPage free to rename its param?' That is exactly an api query -- kind=api query=renderBody -- and I answered it with `grep -n 'function .*:renderBody' *.lua`. The grep was right (10 implementations, and the convention fell straight out: renderers that ignore the ctx write `_`, pages that pass it through write `ctx`), but I never considered map_query, because luacheck had handed me a file:line and my attention was on the line rather than on what the family did. Third session running that the linter's coordinates have crowded out the index. The real gap: shadowing is a question about LOCALS and UPVALUES, and nothing here indexes those. The map covers module api, @field r|w, annotations -- all of which are about what a module exposes, while every one of these 35 judgements was about what a module keeps private and where it collides with itself. The load-bearing lookup was 'which of the ten pages declare a module-level local ctx' -- that is what identified editorPage as the sole outlier and moved the fix off the shared interface and onto the one file that deserved it. I answered it by grepping four files by hand. I am not proposing an index of every local (noise, and the .map would double), but module-level locals specifically are a small, stable set that reads exactly like the alias table the tooling already resolves receivers through -- and 'who else declares this name at module scope' is the query that turns an outlier hunt into one call.

Fourth null running, and the honest reason is that this session's decisive question was not a location question at all. A review finding and docs/trackerManager.md:746 flatly contradicted each other about whether a rebuild follows a flush -- i.e. whether the tail walk or mm's backstop separates a same-pitch collision -- and no index could adjudicate, because a .map is derived from source and agrees with it by construction. It can tell me voicing.nudgeOnsets is called from trackerManager:2646; it cannot tell me whether that line is reached on the flush path, or in what order relative to midiManager:883. I settled it by monkeypatching both voicing entry points and reading the trace, then by counterfactual (disable each layer, observe the seats: both independently sufficient). Nothing static was ever going to answer it, so this is not a defect -- but it is worth logging that the last four sessions' load-bearing lookups were dynamic ordering, private-table dataflow, module-scope locals, and protocol params, and the index models none of them. One CONCRETE and machine-checkable gap did surface, though, and it burned me for real: docs/bridge-cookbook.md told me to address events by .token, tokens were retired on 2026-07-16, and the failure is silent -- tm:assignEvent(nil, ...) no-ops through a flush without complaint, so my first timing run cheerfully reported 0.0ms for an edit that never happened. grep -rn 'token' trackerManager.lua midiManager.lua returns ZERO. That means a checker could diff identifiers appearing in docs/*.md code fences against the symbols the maps already index, and would have flagged every stale recipe in that file the day tokens went. Doc prose is the one artifact here with no derived counterpart, which is exactly why it rots unobserved -- second session running that the load-bearing falsehood lived in a .md.

Third lint pass, third session the maps sat idle: luacheck emits file:line, so the index has nothing to add to a coordinate list. The one real gap is spec-shaped. I found zz_probe2_spec asserts nothing -- no t.eq anywhere, only print -- purely because its 't' import read as unused; it has been running green-by-construction in every suite. Spec maps already index cases; if a case carried its assertion count, map_query could answer 'which specs assert nothing' directly instead of it falling out of an unrelated lint run. Same shape as the last entry: the artifact with no derived counterpart is the one that rots.

First non-null in five, and earned: one map_query resolved seven symbols across tm and mm (strictNextMap|rebuildTails|buildRawScratch|dirtyChan|notesRaw|rebuildPbs|rebuildPCs) in a single call, and the regex alternation over query is what made that one call instead of seven. That is the tool working as advertised. The gap that cost me is a predicate gap, and it is the sharpest instance of the recurring theme yet -- because this time the index's own design doc names the query it cannot serve. design/interval-dirt.md phase 1 hands dirtyChans a widening type (boolean -> interval set) and writes the migration's safety condition as a literal audit instruction: 'One audit needed: nothing may test == true or count entries.' That is precisely un-askable here. kind='reads' query='dirtyChans' would tell me WHO reads it; the load-bearing question was what each reader DOES with the value, and only one of the 45 sites (trackerManager:3214) compares it to a literal. That single site decided the whole commit: it runs before the pipeline, so the widen at :2648 cannot reach it, so the widen is a no-op, so the emission the design wanted in its place has no consumer until phase 6. I got there by grep_window over dirtyChans and reading all 45 hits by eye. A 'reads' entry that recorded the comparison shape -- truthy vs compared-to-literal -- would turn a type-widening audit from a manual sweep into one query, and this is a repo that widens types on purpose: phase 1 did it, phase 5 will do it again to the same variable. Also, unprompted: the tail walk's cost split (sort 40%, nudge 23%, loop 17%, maps 12%) came from a synthetic probe, not any index, and it inverted the design's commit ordering. No tool gap there -- just logging that the decisive number again came from running code rather than reading it.

Declaration lookup was the win: classify*/unlink* -> exact file:line in one call each, which is what located the dead code. I then misused usedby twice and am logging the misuse rather than blaming the tooling, since a defect log with a false defect in it is worse than no entry. usedby is a cross-module reverse index; gm's classify and unlink are module-local `local function`s, so 'no callers found' for unlink was correct-by-contract, not a recall gap -- a bare call carries no receiver for the alias table to resolve. And query='classify' returned DAG.classify's callers while labelling every row 'DAG.classify': the tool was explicit about which symbol it had resolved and I skimmed past the qualifier. No gap to fix; if anything the lesson is that neither orphan this session (gm's classify, tv's overlapBounds) was findable by index at all -- each is a refactor that removed a caller and left the callee, which luacheck answers in one pass and git log -S explains, and neither is the map's job.

The answer to this pass's central question was sitting in the case index and I asked it last. kind='case' query='growNote*' returns seven cases in one call, one named 'growNote past a same-pitch successor authors a tail past, tm clips realised' -- that test name IS the answer to 'what bounds the tail now tv's overlapBounds is dead' (tv authors freely; tm's rebuildTails clips realised at rebuild). Instead I grepped overlapBounds/rowBounds across lua+md, read adjustDurationCore, and inferred it from the ABSENCE of a clamp -- slower, and an absence is precisely what I could have missed. Lesson for me rather than a defect: when the question is 'what is the live behaviour', spec case names are the cheapest index here and I reach for them last, probably because the kind is called 'case' and I read that as 'test plumbing' rather than 'behaviour, in English'. Genuine limit: no index explains why a callee outlived its caller -- git log -S did (e11cbcf removed the last two call sites, left the function). Richard naming rebuildTails in chat still beat every tool I have.

bypassed the map tooling entirely. The session's one real navigation question was 'who calls voicing.nudgeOnsets' before deleting it, and the answer had to span .lua AND the prose (docs/, design/) that names it -- map_query indexes code only, so one ripgrep over *.lua+*.md beat any sequence of map calls, and it caught three stale doc references I then had to fix. Deleting a public function is a common shape; a usedby that flagged prose mentions, even unresolved filenames, would make it a map question. Everything else was 'read this known function' -- multi_read's job, not the map's.

second commit of the session; bypassed the maps again, and this time usedby genuinely could have answered the question -- 'who calls util.pick' -- so the gap is payload, not recall. I needed each call site's ARGUMENT (which pass hoisted constants, which pass literals, which are hot), and usedby returns edges, not call text; grep gave me the 15 sites and their key strings in one shot, which is the whole question when deciding whether to change a shared signature. A usedby that could echo the matched call line would flip this class of lookup back to the map. Also note git log -S carried the session's other decisive lookup (when did buildRawScratch land) -- history, not structure, and rightly not the map's job.

phase 4.5 commits 2-3; every target came with a file:line anchor carried over from the prior session's summary, so multiread/grep_window ranges covered it all and the maps were never consulted -- anchor-rich resumed sessions are simply not the map's regime

phase 4.5 commit 4; one grep over the .map re-anchored every target post-compaction, and the # Fields index (detune r/w sites) surfaced the pbs nil-detune hazard the dead scratch had been masking -- exactly the field-shaped question the index is for

design session for phase 4.75 + the seed-dirt inversion; usedby module=intervals returned the module's entire production surface (7 sites, spec included) in one call -- the retirement plan fell straight out of it

phase 4.75 commit 1; map_query kind=fn re-anchored the design doc's stale line numbers (seedAt/absorbReloadDirt/rebuildTails/exciseNotes) to current sites in one call. no defect -- grep_window was for verifying no other dirtyChans reader iterates it as intervals, which the index can't answer
phase 4.75 commit 2 (pure extraction inside one fn); map_query query=rebuildTails gave the exact line range in one call, all the work was inside that window. nothing the index missed

phase 4.75 commit 3 (fixtures + shadow-walk doc). map_query nailed the spec idiom lookup, but verifying the post-edit hook actually regenerated the map for the new symbols is a grep against map/*.map -- no tool surface answers 'did the index pick up seekTails yet'

phase 4.75 commit 4 (retire intervals, linear walk authoritative). map_query kind=fn with an alternation regex returned all four walk fns with exact line ranges in one call, feeding multiread precisely. Only bypass was grep over design/*.md for section headings -- design docs aren't mapped, expected.

phase 4.75 commit 5 (frontier walk + shadow). map_query kind=fn alternation pulled mergeIndexed/walkable/rawNotes/byUuid in one call for multiread. Bypasses were single-symbol definition lookups (rawThenLogical, voicing.separateOnset) where grep beats a round-trip, and the O(n^2) wholesale diagnosis came from reading linearTails -- no index surface answers a perf-shape question.

the threshold flip -- went around the maps entirely, knowing the tail-walk region from the prior session; grep for function names + Read was enough. A reads/writes query scoping pitch/evType across note vs pb/cc records might have surfaced the nil-pitch seed hazard before the flip exposed it, but I didn't think to ask the index a field-shape question mid-refactor.

map_query nailed the fx/park/pbs fn lookups and the dirtyChans call-site sweep via grep_window; the one thing it does not answer is pipeline stage *ordering* (which stage calls which, in rebuild()) — that stayed a grep.

map_query kind='api' surfaced tm:getChannel and the tail/park fns in one call, which unlocked live bridge inspection; the actual diagnosis lived in reaper_eval (perf tree, call counts) that the maps can't cover. No index miss — the fx->tails noteLive shape I confirmed by reading source, and a kind='shape' query on noteLive entries might have saved that Read.
query expandChannel found nothing — it is a local nested inside rebuildFx, below map granularity; had to query the fx stage fns and read the whole range
fields query for ccExisting/noteExisting (locals passed as params) found nothing -- param flows are below map granularity; a plain grep answered in one call
bypassed maps: targets were known from the design doc + prior session, so grep_window windows beat map lookups; nothing the index could have answered faster this time
needed the stage a perf.start('ccs')/'fx' literal wraps -- string-literal span sites are a grep job, not a map query; worked first try, no defect

map_query kind=reads for 'columns.notes'/'col.events' returned nothing -- nested container fields aren't indexed like scalar fields (.ppqL works); and kind=fn 'notesRaw' in midiManager missed the method. Both sent me to grep. But this task was mostly behavioural tracing (which writer disorders a lane) that no index answers.

map_query 'expandChannel' returned no matches -- it's a local closure inside rebuildFx, and nested @fn locals aren't indexed like top-level functions. grep_window found it plus the parkRegions precedent in one window; design doc carried the target so cost was low.
bypassed maps: post-compaction summary carried exact line anchors into trackerManager, so grep_window/multi_read were direct; no structural query needed

Bypassed the maps: every target was already named in conversation (projectEvent, rebuildInternals, the flip loop), so grep_window/multi_read gave exact windows in one call each. The one question maps could not have answered anyway: who consumes the `internal` local — file-local data flow, not module surface; grep was the only tool. No defect to report, just a session shaped like known-target range reads.
Packer port: every target already line-anchored from the prior slice; multiread/grep_window sufficed, maps never consulted

map_query nailed rebuildRegionPark in one call; the rest was line-level dataflow tracing (which lists feed which scan, loc<->ppq ordering, mm rawInChan internals) that lives below the map granularity — expected, not a defect. grep_window+multiread carried it well.

map_query case-filter on the spec map pinned the PA-park test in one call. No map surfaces a reindex-timing invariant (is mm's index dense here?) -- that lives only in leaveNest's prose, reached by reading. Expected, below map granularity.

Tiny additive-field commit (snapshot enrichment). grep_window confirmed evt.cc/evType field names against route-by-window sites in one shot. map_query usedby correctly reported no callers for a file-local (snapshot) -- right answer, but told me nothing I could act on; grep did the field-name verification the maps can't (field values, not declarations).

map_query module='specs' returned 'no .map files matched' -- the spec-map module filter wants the spec's own name, not the specs/ dir; fell back to ls+read to find the cc-gate spec. grep_window one-shot the file-local cc splice helpers (bodies + line numbers) that map_query fn-kind would only name, not show.

kind='fields' query='cells' module='trackerView' jumped straight to the B1 cell-carry build sites -- the session's root cause -- in one call; would have been a long grep sweep. Weak spot: tracing 'how does the patternEditor delete a breakpoint' is a flow question across pe->cmgr->tv that no kind expresses; fell back to raw grep on patternEditor.lua.

read the spec .map first -- its # Cases index showed case 2 already covered kept/clean/orphan cc producers, so I extended that case instead of authoring a new spec. Source nav went through grep_window not map_query: the function names (buildCcExistingInWindows, spliceChannelCCs, expandChannel) were already in context from the prior session, so a structural query would have been a detour, not a defect.

spec map's # Cases/# Helpers index again earned its keep (assertParity + keep-pin pattern found without reading source blind). Rest was grep + ranged reads on trackerManager -- targets were named in the design doc, so no structural query needed. One friction: delay's milli-QN unit cost two probe rounds; a map_query 'fields' on delay wouldn't have said units, only timing.lua's contract line did.
kind=fields on ^sample$ gave the full read/write census in one call, incl. the spec pin I had to flip; spec map # Cases index named the old no-inheritance guard before I read any spec source

kind=fields census of ppqL in midiManager returned nothing (mm stores it, tm reads cc.ppqL) — the sidecar rides events, not declared fields, so the field index was blind; found the projectEvent ppqL-drop by reading source. grep_window carried the rest well.

usedby on tm:rebuild truncated at 60 hits, nearly all spec call sites — a way to exclude specs (or group by file) would have answered 'which production modules call this' in one look instead of eyeballing past the flood.
